key_insights.insight: People develop a 'contribution score' based on the value they bring to conversations, influencing how others perceive and listen to them.
key_insights.quote: Like that individual, whenever he spoke, I would see the person sat next to him,who I won't name, almost like low-key roll their eyes in the first five seconds and shut it down before he'd even got it out.

key_insights.insight: It's crucial to address disrespectful behavior or boundary violations immediately to prevent them from recurring and fostering resentment.
key_insights.quote: As soon as it happens, you have to address it.

key_insights.insight: When someone doesn't listen to you, first assess your own communication style, including paralinguistics like tone and projection.
key_insights.quote: When you do speak, do you look at people when you speak? Do you project your voice?

key_insights.insight: It's often better to remain silent in meetings unless you have something valuable to contribute.
key_insights.quote: Do you have something beneficial to say or value to say? Because if you don't, don't say anything. Half the meetings I go to, I don't speak because maybe I have nothing to say.

key_insights.insight: When a team member makes a mistake, approach the situation by understanding their perspective and offering support.
key_insights.quote: Is there anything I can do to help make your job better so you can be more successful at what you do?

key_insights.insight: When faced with disrespect, first reflect on whether you have inadvertently set standards that allow such behavior.
key_insights.quote: What have you done to let people think that they can do that to you? What standards have you created or what things have you set up to let people think, I don't have to deliver on time. I can't be disrespectful. I can't show up late for work.

key_insights.insight: Effective leaders establish clear boundaries and expectations from the beginning and then gradually relax, rather than trying to impose them later.
key_insights.quote: It is easier to be more, have boundaries and be a little bit more sturdy and more authoritative in the beginning and then pull back and to be everybody's friend and then try to put those boundaries in place.

actionable_advice.advice: Evaluate and adjust your paralinguistic communication cues.
actionable_advice.how_to: Record yourself speaking, paying attention to tone, pitch, eye contact, and voice projection. Practice varying these elements to increase engagement.

actionable_advice.advice: Actively manage your 'contribution score' in meetings.
actionable_advice.how_to: Before speaking, ask yourself if your contribution will add value. If not, remain silent. Focus on quality over quantity in your remarks.

actionable_advice.advice: When addressing disrespect, initiate a dialogue with the offending party.
actionable_advice.how_to: Instead of immediately confronting, ask them to explain their actions. Listen actively and empathetically to understand their perspective before addressing the issue.

actionable_advice.advice: Set clear expectations and boundaries at the outset of any professional relationship.
actionable_advice.how_to: Clearly communicate performance standards, acceptable behavior, and consequences for violations. Be firm and consistent in enforcing these boundaries.

actionable_advice.advice: If conflict arises, approach it as a debate not a personal attack.
actionable_advice.how_to: Maintain a calm and respectful tone, focus on the issues at hand, and aim to find a mutually acceptable solution.

memorable_stories: The anecdote about the person in the New York office who made irrelevant suggestions during brainstorms, lowering his 'contribution score'.
The anecdote about the person named Paul in the UK office who barely spoke but when he did everyone listened and gave his contributions the floor
Story about Evie being happy being the 'dumbest' person in the room because she gets to learn from the smart people in the room.
contrarian_claims: The idea that continually speaking in meetings to 'make your voice heard' is counterproductive if you have nothing valuable to say.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Moment 182: How To Make People Respect You In Seconds: Evy Poumpouras
